Overall, thorough sample preparation is essential in XPS to minimize artifacts and ensure the accuracy of surface chemical analysis [190].

Nazri et al. synthesized CQDs and used XPS to get a thorough compositional characterization. The survey spectrum is shown in Fig. 8
a, where three distinct peaks at 284 eV (C1s), 396 eV (N1s), and 528 eV (O1s) are visible. These peaks indicate that the CQDs include components of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. The C1s location was used to calibrate the XPS spectra in Fig. 8 b. This position broke down into three peaks at 281.7, 283.5, and 284.6 eV, which represent C = O, C–C, and C = C, respectively. Nitrogen atoms in H-N-C2 were revealed by the N1s spectra (Fig. 8 c) at 397.1 eV and N2− at 399.5 eV. Two strong bands at 528.9 and 529.6 eV, which correspond to lattice oxygen and C = O binding energies, respectively, were seen in the O1s spectra shown in Fig. 8 d. This thorough XPS investigation revealed important details on the chemical states and composition of the produced CQDs [191].

UV–visible spectroscopy is a powerful technique used to analyze the electronic structure of materials, and it finds application in the study of CQDs. In UV–visible spectroscopy, the absorption of ultraviolet and visible light by a material is measured, providing information about the electronic transitions within the material [192]. For CQDs, their UV–visible absorption spectra reveal details about the band structure and energy levels associated with the electronic transitions in the quantum dots. The absorption peaks in the UV–visible region are attributed to the π-π* transitions of the carbon-based systems present in the CQDs [193]. The size, shape, and surface functionalization of CQDs significantly influence their optical properties, as reflected in the UV–visible spectra. As the size of CQDs decreases, the quantum confinement effect becomes more pronounced, leading to shifts in absorption peaks. Additionally, surface functional groups can introduce energy levels and modify the electronic structure, impacting the absorption characteristics of CQDs. The UV–visible spectroscopy of CQDs can be used to determine their optical bandgap, which is crucial for understanding their potential applications in optoelectronic devices [188].

Sometimes a mismatch in the UV–visible absorbance and PL excitation spectra is observed. The discrepancy between UV–visible absorbance and PL excitation spectra can occur due to several factors: Ⅰ) Excitation Wavelength: UV–visible absorbance spectroscopy measures the absorption of photons by a material across a range of wavelengths, while PL excitation spectroscopy measures the absorption of photons that excite luminescence emission. The excitation wavelength used in PL spectroscopy might differ from the range covered in UV–visible absorbance spectroscopy, leading to variations in observed spectra. Ⅱ) Electronic Transitions: UV–visible absorbance spectroscopy primarily detects electronic transitions between energy levels in the ground and excited states. In contrast, PL excitation spectroscopy involves transitions between excited states, which may not always correspond directly to transitions observed in absorbance spectra. Ⅲ) Energy Loss: In PL excitation spectroscopy, some of the absorbed energy may be lost through non-radiative processes such as phonon relaxation, leading to differences in the observed spectra compared to absorbance spectroscopy. Ⅳ) Surface Effects: PL excitation spectroscopy is more sensitive to surface effects and defects compared to UV–visible absorbance spectroscopy. Surface states and defects can lead to additional spectral features in PL excitation spectra that are not present in absorbance spectra. Ⅴ) Instrumental Factors: Differences in instrumentation, such as the resolution and sensitivity of the detectors used in UV–visible absorbance and PL excitation spectroscopy, can also contribute to discrepancies between the spectra.

The bandgap information aids in assessing the semiconducting nature of CQDs and their suitability for various electronic applications. Furthermore, UV–visible spectroscopy is valuable for monitoring the stability and interactions of CQDs in different environments. Changes in the absorption spectra may indicate variations in size, aggregation, or surface chemistry, providing insights into the behavior of CQDs under different conditions [194]. Determining the bandgap of CQDs through UV–visible spectroscopy entails scrutinizing the absorption spectrum of the CQDs and discerning the onset of absorption, denoted as the absorption edge, which corresponds to the bandgap energy. A systematic procedure is indispensable for this purpose. Initially, the CQDs solution is meticulously prepared in a suitable solvent, ensuring optimal dispersion and the absence of impurities. Subsequently, UV–visible spectrophotometry is employed to meticulously record the absorption spectrum, encompassing wavelengths from ultraviolet to visible light. Upon acquisition of the absorption spectrum, meticulous analysis ensues. The bandgap energy manifests at the wavelength where absorption commences to rise sharply, indicative of the absorption edge [194]. For enhanced precision, a Tauc plot is constructed. This plot juxtaposes (αhν)2 against photon energy (hν), wherein α represents the absorption coefficient and hν denotes photon energy. The interception of the tangent line with the energy axis at the steepest region of the curve divulges the bandgap energy. Following the identification of the absorption edge or derivation of the bandgap energy from the Tauc plot, calculation ensues utilizing the equation:
Eg = h c / λ
Here, Eg​ denotes the bandgap energy, h signifies Planck's constant, c embodies the speed of light, and λ represents the wavelength corresponding to the absorption edge [195].

Using the heat synthesis approach, Fawaz et al. were able to effectively synthesize CQDs. They then used a UV–Vis spectrophotometer to analyze the physical and chemical characteristics of the CQDs. A detailed analysis of the carbon dots' absorption spectra was performed (Fig. 9 ). At 280 nm, there was a noticeable peak that was identified as the π-π* orbital transition linked to the sp2 hybridization pattern of the carbon–carbon double bonds (C = C). A shoulder was also observed at around 360 nm, which is the n-π* orbital transition with the sp3 hybridization pattern. This finding suggests that bonds can form between carbon atoms and other functional groups including oxygen and nitrogen atoms [196].

XRD analysis is frequently employed to probe the structural characteristics of CQDs. Typically, XRD patterns of CQDs exhibit broad peaks, indicating a high degree of disorder in the arrangement of carbon atoms on their surfaces. These broad peaks suggest the amorphous nature of CQDs and reflect the cluster-like structure of their surfaces [197]. Furthermore, XRD is instrumental in determining the crystalline phases of CQDs synthesized using various precursors and methods, providing valuable information about their particle size, phase purity, and crystal structure. In several studies, researchers have utilized XRD to investigate the surface nature and crystalline phases of synthesized CQDs [198]. In one study by Yang et al., XRD analysis revealed the clustered nature of the CQDs' surface. The broad peak observed in the XRD pattern indicated a high degree of disorder in the arrangement of carbon atoms on the CQDs' surface. Additionally, the deviation of the crystalline peak of chitosan at 2θ = 20 to 23° suggested the formation of CQDs with an amorphous carbon phase [199]. Another study by Dong et al. confirmed the formation of amorphous carbon materials using XRD analysis. A broad peak centered at 22.2° indicated the presence of disordered carbon atoms in the synthesized CQDs [200]. Thulasi et al. investigated CQDs synthesized from vehicle exhaust soot using nitric acid via a one-pot acid reflux method. The XRD pattern exhibited a sharp peak at 2θ = 29°, indicating the presence of carbon atoms arranged in an unsymmetrical manner and the amorphous nature of the synthesized CQDs. Additionally, small peaks observed around 2θ = 32, 35, 38, 42, and 48° suggested the presence of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the CQDs [201]. Mao et al. developed photoluminescent carbon dots from glycerol through pyrolysis of poly(acrylic acid). XRD analysis revealed a wide peak near 2θ = 24°, confirming the graphite structure of the carbon dots [202]. Generally, XRD analysis serves as an effective method for characterizing the structural properties, crystalline phases, and surface nature of CQDs synthesized through various methods.

Kechagias et al. conducted a study focused on the synthesis of CQDs. The characterization of the final product involved various instrumental and chemical techniques, including XRD analysis. The XRD diffractogram of the N/S-Carbon QDs (Fig. 10 ) revealed two broad peaks indicative of the amorphous phase of graphitic carbon structure, along with peaks corresponding to the Fm-3 m cubic calcium crystal structure, suggesting the presence of calcium particles in the synthesized material [203].

Raman spectroscopy is a non-destructive and versatile analytical tool widely employed for investigating the vibrational modes of molecules, furnishing intricate details about their molecular structure and elemental composition. When applied to CQDs, Raman spectroscopy emerges as an indispensable method, offering multifaceted insights into various aspects of these nanostructures. Specifically, it elucidates the surface morphology and chemistry, providing a comprehensive understanding of the intricate surface structure of CQDs. Additionally, Raman spectroscopy unveils crucial information regarding the optical properties of CQDs [197]. Furthermore, this technique unravels the crystalline nature of CQDs, delineating the degree of structural order and crystallinity within these nanomaterials. In the case of CQDs, Raman spectroscopy provides a means to probe their surface structure and identify different vibrational modes. Typically, CQDs exhibit two main bands in Raman spectra: the D band and the G band. The D band, observed near 1332 cm−1, is attributed to highly disordered sp3 hybridized carbon atoms on the CQD surface. This band indicates the presence of defects or disorder within the carbon structure. On the other hand, the G band, observed near 1582 cm−1, is associated with sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the CQDs, indicative of graphitic or crystalline carbon structures [204]. By analyzing the intensities and positions of these bands, researchers can gain insights into the degree of disorder, crystallinity, and surface chemistry of CQDs. This information is crucial for understanding and optimizing the properties of CQDs for various applications, including optoelectronics, sensing, and biomedical imaging.

In Raman spectroscopy, firstly, the sample must be cleaned thoroughly to remove any contaminants that could interfere with the Raman signal. This typically involves washing with solvents such as ethanol or acetone followed by drying. For solid samples, they may need to be ground into a fine powder to ensure uniformity and maximize surface area for interaction with the laser. Liquid samples may require dilution or filtration to remove particulates. Additionally, samples may need to be mounted on a suitable substrate or placed in a suitable container to facilitate handling and measurement [205].

NMR spectroscopy is a useful tool for probing the structure of molecules, including carbon-based materials like quantum dots. In the case of CQDs, NMR can provide valuable insights into their composition and bonding patterns.

13C NMR Spectroscopy: This technique allows for the characterization of carbon atoms within the quantum dots. By analyzing the chemical shifts of carbon nuclei, one can distinguish between different types of carbon atoms based on their bonding environments. In the case of CQDs, Tian et al. utilized 13C NMR to analyze the hybrid types of carbon atoms present in the crystalline network. Aromatic (sp2) carbons typically exhibit resonances in the region extending from 90 to 180 ppm, while aliphatic (sp3) carbons resonate in the region extending from 8 to 80 ppm. By analyzing the peaks in the NMR spectrum, one can determine the relative abundance of sp2 and sp3 carbons in the carbon dots. In this study, the absence of aliphatic carbons below 120 ppm indicated the dominance of aromatic carbons in the carbon dots. Additionally, a sequence of peaks between 120 and 150 ppm further confirmed the presence of aromatic carbons [206].

1H NMR Spectroscopy: This technique focuses on the hydrogen nuclei within the quantum dots. By analyzing the chemical shifts of hydrogen atoms, one can gain insights into the types of chemical groups present in the material. In the case of CQDs, the proton spectrum revealed the presence of various functional groups, including aliphatic C–H resonances, CH–O, and CH–N groups. The signals observed in the spectrum provided information about the chemical environment and bonding patterns of hydrogen atoms within the carbon dots [198].

Sousa and colleagues pioneered the creation of CQDs from readily available, locally abundant wet pomace (WP), a semi-solid residue from olive oil production. The 1H/13C NMR spectra of WP-CDs-5 (Fig. 11 ) reveal distinct features. In the 13C NMR spectrum, three primary regions emerge: one, spanning approximately 20–40 ppm, showcases aliphatic sp3-carbons; another, from 55 to 90 ppm, encompasses C-sp3 signals associated with heteroatoms (N/O), potentially including C-sp2/C-sp resonances beyond 75 ppm. The third region, notably between 175 and 190 ppm, indicates various carboxyl-derived carbons (N-C = O, O-C = O, CO2−), likely linked to aromatic structures within the carbon nanoparticles. The 1H NMR spectrum supports these findings, featuring characteristic proton signals from saturated carbons (0.7–2.8 ppm), with signals above ∼ 1.8 ppm possibly corresponding to aromatic components or C–H resonances in β-carbons of saturated N/O heterocycles. Protons linked to heteroatoms (N/O) are observed in the 3.25–4.5 ppm range. Together, these regions encompass nearly 90 % of the quantifiable protons in the 1H NMR spectrum [207].

Zeta potential analysis is integral to understanding the behavior of CQDs when dispersed in a solution. Zeta potential, defined as the measure of electrostatic repulsion between similarly charged particles in a dispersed system, provides crucial information about the stability of the dispersion. For CQDs, which are often dispersed in solvents for various applications, assessing their zeta potential offers insights into the electrostatic interactions between individual quantum dots and how these interactions influence the overall stability of the dispersion [208]. Stability criteria based on zeta potential serve as a guideline for determining whether a dispersion of nanoparticles will remain homogeneous or undergo aggregation. In the case of CQDs, nanoparticles with a zeta potential more positive than + 30 mV or more negative than −30 mV are considered stable. This threshold indicates that the electrostatic repulsion between particles is sufficient to prevent aggregation. Thus, maintaining a zeta potential within this range is crucial for ensuring the stability of carbon quantum dot dispersions over time [209]. The relationship between zeta potential and stability is straightforward: a higher magnitude of zeta potential corresponds to greater stability. A high zeta potential signifies strong electrostatic repulsion between particles, which effectively prevents them from aggregating or flocculating. Conversely, a low zeta potential suggests weaker repulsion, increasing the likelihood of particle aggregation. For CQDs, maintaining a high zeta potential is desirable to preserve their characteristics and prevent undesired changes in their properties [210]. As a result, zeta potential analysis offers valuable insights into the stability and dispersion behavior of CQDs in solution. By understanding the electrostatic interactions between particles, researchers can optimize synthesis methods and surface modifications to achieve desired stability and enhance the performance of CQDs in various applications.

Pete et al. synthesized CQDs and conducted zeta analysis to comprehensively evaluate their properties. Through zeta potential measurements, they aimed to assess the stability of the CQDs when in colloidal or soluble form. Their findings, as illustrated in Fig. 12 , revealed a zeta potential value of −20.8 mV, suggesting a moderate level of stability for the nanoparticles under investigation [211].

AFM, developed by G. Binnig et al. in 1986, is a powerful scanning probe technique that enables imaging with nanometer-scale resolution [212]. In AFM, a cantilever with a sharp tip is utilized to scan across the surface of a sample. The interaction between the tip and the surface causes the cantilever to deflect, and this deflection is measured to map the surface topography. The setup of an AFM typically involves a cantilever tip attached to a piezo-actuator, which allows precise scanning motion. To detect the deflections of the cantilever, an optical lever method is commonly employed. This method involves focusing a laser beam onto the back side of the cantilever and collecting the reflected beam with a photodiode. The deflections of the laser beam are then monitored and used in a feedback loop to control the force and position of the tip during scanning. AFM can operate in different modes, each offering unique advantages: a) Contact mode: In this mode, the tip continuously interacts with the surface, providing high-resolution images of surface topography. b) Non-contact mode: Here, the tip oscillates above the surface, and the forces between the tip and the sample are measured without direct contact. This mode is suitable for delicate samples or when minimizing surface damage is critical. c) Tapping mode: Also known as intermittent contact mode, the tip intermittently contacts the surface, oscillating close to its resonance frequency. This mode reduces lateral forces and is commonly used for imaging soft or fragile samples [213].

In the context of CQDs, AFM can provide valuable insight into their morphology, size distribution, and surface characteristics at the nanoscale. By imaging CQDs with AFM, researchers can observe individual dots, determine their size and shape, and investigate their aggregation behavior. Additionally, AFM can be used to study the interaction between CQDs and other materials, such as substrates or biomolecules, providing crucial information for various applications ranging from nanoelectronics to biomedicine [15].

Kechagias et al. synthesized CQDs, with a focus on their nitrogen and sulfur-containing variants (N/S-Carbon QDs). To assess their physical characteristics, AFM was employed for precise measurements. The AFM images presented in Fig. 13 vividly depict the spherical nature of the N/S-Carbon QDs, showcasing diameters ranging from 5 to 8 nm, with an average diameter calculated at 6.56 nm. This observation serves to validate their distinct “quantum” morphology. Notably, the thickness measurements revealed intriguing variations, with recorded values of 5.362, 7.633, 7.923, and 8.026 nm. These measurements surpass the average thickness of 3 nm previously reported for N-doped Carbon QDs, indicating unique structural characteristics for the N/S-Carbon QDs synthesized in this study [203].
Here is a comparison table (Table 3 ) for the characterization techniques of CQDs:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of bioimaging applications of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Recent scientific advances were obtained in physics, mathematics, biology, chemistry, and materials science [214–217]. The advances made in the field of bioimaging are also very significant which will be examined below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages and disadvantages of using CQDs in bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs are generally non-toxic and exhibit low cytotoxicity, making them suitable for use in biological systems without causing harm to living cells. This biocompatibility is crucial for applications such as in vivo imaging, where the material must interact seamlessly with biological environments [218]. Additionally, CQDs possess remarkable optical properties that contribute to their effectiveness in bioimaging. They exhibit strong and tunable fluorescence, allowing for sensitive detection and imaging of biological structures. The fluorescence emission of CQDs can be easily adjusted by controlling their size, surface functionalization, and chemical composition, providing versatility in designing probes for specific imaging applications. This tunability enables researchers to tailor CQDs for optimal performance in a variety of biological contexts [219]. Another advantage of CQDs in bioimaging is their ease of synthesis and functionalization. CQDs can be synthesized using relatively simple and cost-effective methods, and their surface chemistry can be modified to enhance their stability, water solubility, and targeting capabilities. This flexibility in functionalization allows for the design of CQDs with specific properties, enabling targeted imaging of particular biomolecules or cellular structures [220]. CQDs also demonstrate good photostability, meaning they can withstand prolonged exposure to light without significant degradation of their fluorescence. This property is crucial for maintaining the accuracy and reliability of imaging data over extended periods, making CQDs suitable for time-lapse and long-term imaging studies [221]. Furthermore, CQDs possess a high surface area and can be easily integrated into various matrices, such as hydrogels or nanoparticles, for enhanced stability and functionality. This feature makes them adaptable to different imaging modalities and applications, ranging from simple fluorescence microscopy to advanced imaging techniques, such as photoacoustic imaging and multimodal imaging [97]. In summary, the advantages of using CQDs in bioimaging include their excellent biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence, ease of synthesis and functionalization, photostability, and versatility in integration with different matrices. These properties collectively position CQDs as valuable tools for advancing the field of bioimaging, offering researchers a powerful and customizable platform for visualizing and studying biological processes at the nanoscale.

While CQDs exhibit several advantages for bioimaging, it's essential to consider certain disadvantages and challenges associated with their use in this context. One notable limitation is the potential for limited tissue penetration depth in imaging applications. The strong fluorescence exhibited by CQDs may be advantageous for sensitivity, but it can also lead to increased background signal and reduced imaging depth, particularly in tissues with higher levels of autofluorescence [30]. Another challenge is the potential for rapid clearance from the body, which may limit their effectiveness for long-term in vivo imaging studies. The renal clearance of smaller-sized CQDs can result in a shorter circulation time, potentially impacting their ability to accumulate in specific tissues for extended observation periods. This limitation may require additional modifications or strategies to enhance the pharmacokinetics and retention of CQDs in target tissues. The toxicity of CQDs is a concern that needs careful consideration. While CQDs are generally regarded as biocompatible, variations in synthesis methods and surface functionalization can influence their toxicity. It is crucial to thoroughly assess the potential cytotoxic effects and long-term impact on biological systems, especially when considering clinical applications [222]. The potential for aggregation of CQDs is another challenge that may affect their performance in bioimaging. Aggregation can lead to changes in the optical properties of CQDs, affecting their fluorescence intensity and spectral characteristics. Strategies to prevent or minimize aggregation, such as appropriate surface modifications, must be carefully implemented to ensure the reliability and accuracy of imaging results [223]. Additionally, the scalability of CQD production for large-scale applications remains a challenge. Ensuring reproducibility and consistency in the synthesis of CQDs is essential for their widespread use in bioimaging and other biomedical applications. Overcoming these challenges may involve the development of standardized synthesis protocols and quality control measures [32]. In conclusion, while CQDs offer significant advantages for bioimaging, including biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence, and ease of functionalization, it is crucial to address potential disadvantages such as limited tissue penetration, rapid clearance, toxicity concerns, aggregation issues, and the scalability of production. A thorough understanding of these challenges will contribute to the continued improvement and successful implementation of CQDs in bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Types of imaging methods with CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Imaging methods using CQDs typically leverage their fluorescence properties for detection and visualization. Here are some common types of imaging methods with CQDs:

Fluorescence imaging using CQDs has emerged as a promising and versatile technique in various scientific and biomedical applications. CQDs are nanoscale carbon-based materials with unique optical properties, including strong fluorescence, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity. These characteristics make them ideal candidates for use as contrast agents in fluorescence imaging [224]. One of the key advantages of CQDs in fluorescence imaging is their tunable fluorescence emission. Researchers can manipulate the size, surface functionalization, and composition of CQDs to tailor their optical properties for specific imaging applications. This tunability allows for the generation of fluorescence signals across a wide range of wavelengths, enabling the detection of diverse biological targets [225]. In the field of biomedical imaging, CQDs have shown great potential for use in live-cell imaging and in vivo imaging. Their biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity make them suitable for tracking and imaging biological structures and processes without causing harm to living cells. Additionally, the ability of CQDs to penetrate cell membranes enhances their utility in cellular imaging studies [226]. The unique surface chemistry of CQDs also facilitates the conjugation of targeting ligands, such as antibodies or peptides, onto their surfaces. This functionalization enables the specific targeting of particular cells or biomolecules, enhancing the precision and specificity of fluorescence imaging. These targeted approaches are particularly valuable in cancer diagnostics and therapy, where the ability to selectively image and treat cancer cells is crucial [227]. Moreover, CQDs exhibit excellent photostability, which means that their fluorescence properties remain constant over prolonged periods of illumination. This stability is essential for acquiring high-quality imaging data, especially in time-lapse studies and long-term observations [228].

In summary, fluorescence imaging with CQDs represents a cutting-edge and versatile approach in scientific and biomedical research. The tunable fluorescence emission, biocompatibility, and functionalization capabilities of CQDs make them valuable tools for a wide range of applications, from cellular imaging to targeted diagnostics and therapeutic interventions. As technology continues to advance, the integration of CQDs into imaging platforms holds great promise for furthering our understanding of complex biological systems and improving medical diagnostics and treatments.

Yu et al. developed new N-doped yellow fluorescent nano-carbon dots (Y-CDs) by a simple hydrothermal process employing urea and L-tartaric acid as precursors. The produced Y-CDs showed strong fluorescence, a high quantum yield of 15.9 %, and photostability across a range of circumstances, in contrast to earlier carbon dots that had blue fluorescence and a low quantum yield. The Y-CDs were effectively used as imaging probes for MDA-MB-231 cells and showed efficient and selective cobalamin detection. Before performing cell imaging, the CCK-8 (Cell Counting Kit-8) technique was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the Y-CDs to MDA-MB-231 cells. The cell survival rate following four hours of co-culturing with varying concentrations of Y-CD is displayed in Fig. 14
a. Interestingly, a dose of 20 mg/mL produced a cell survival rate that was more than 90 %, which led to its selection for further imaging studies. Cell sites are shown by the blue fluorescence that DAPI dye emits when excited by a 441 nm laser, as seen in Fig. 14 b, c. Following an hour of cell incubation, Y-CDs penetrated the nucleus and showed a little yellow fluorescence when illuminated by a 490 nm laser. The yellow fluorescence became more intense after 4 h, indicating that Y-CDs were well-suited for cell imaging applications and that they had successfully entered the nucleus [229].

Photoacoustic imaging (PAI) is a biomedical imaging technique that integrates ultrasound and laser-induced photoacoustic effects. It relies on the generation of ultrasound waves through the absorption of laser light by biological tissues or contrast agents. CQDs have garnered attention in the realm of photoacoustic imaging due to their unique properties [230]. CQDs exhibit distinctive optical characteristics, including strong absorption in the near-infrared (NIR) region. This property is advantageous for deep tissue penetration and reduced scattering, making them suitable for in vivo imaging. In the context of PAI, CQDs serve as contrast agents, enhancing photoacoustic signals in biological tissues [231]. Their high sensitivity in the NIR range allows for improved detection of signals, and their biocompatibility is often a key factor in their use for in vivo applications. One of the significant advantages of utilizing CQDs in photoacoustic imaging is their ability to reduce background signals. The NIR light they absorb penetrates deeper into tissues than visible light, resulting in improved imaging depth and resolution [232]. Additionally, CQDs can be engineered to possess multifunctional properties, such as drug delivery or targeting, in addition to their imaging capabilities [233]. However, challenges exist in the widespread adoption of CQDs for photoacoustic imaging. Ensuring the biocompatibility and minimizing the toxicity of CQDs are critical considerations. Researchers also need to optimize various properties of CQDs, such as size, surface functionalization, and absorption characteristics, to tailor them for specific imaging applications [234]. Ongoing research in the field focuses on addressing these challenges and further refining the use of CQDs in photoacoustic imaging. The ultimate goal is to enhance the capabilities of this imaging modality for diverse biomedical applications, including early disease detection and monitoring treatment responses.

Zhang et al employed a molecular fusion approach to design and synthesize a novel series of nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs). These synthesized N-CQDs exhibited a narrow size distribution, with an average size of approximately 1.50 nm. The optical properties of the N-CQDs were adjustable by varying the mass ratio of the precursor and catalyst. Furthermore, these N-CQDs were utilized as bioimaging probes, showcasing rapid imaging velocity, swift metabolism, and a significant enhancement of the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, resulting in improved accumulation in tumor regions. This study provides a roadmap for the development of advanced N-CQDs for both fluorescence and photoacoustic imaging. By adopting this strategy, the N-CQDs have the potential to exhibit optimal fluorescence intensity and photoacoustic efficiency, enabling more comprehensive, high-quality, and accurate in vivo tumor imaging [235].

ce:section.ce:section-title: MRI contrast Enhancement and multimodal imaging approaches with CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) stands as a powerful medical imaging modality, leveraging the magnetic properties of tissues to generate detailed images of the human body. However, the inherent contrast between different tissues can limit MRI's ability to effectively differentiate between them [236]. To address this challenge and enhance the visibility of specific tissues or lesions, contrast agents are frequently employed in conjunction with MRI. CQDs have emerged as promising candidates for MRI contrast agents, showcasing unique properties that offer potential advantages over traditional contrast agents. CQDs exhibit size-dependent optical and magnetic characteristics, and they can be tailored to emit light across a broad spectrum of wavelengths [237]. Moreover, CQDs can undergo functionalization with various molecules, enabling them to achieve biocompatibility and target specific tissues or cells. The potential advantages of CQDs over traditional contrast agents include their small size, which allows for enhanced penetration into cells and tissues. Their tunable properties, encompassing a variety of sizes, shapes, and surface chemistries, offer precise control over their optical and magnetic characteristics [238]. Furthermore, CQDs can be functionalized with biocompatible polymers like PEG (polyethylene glycol) to mitigate toxicity and prolong their circulation time within the body. The multifunctionality of CQDs is another noteworthy aspect, as they can be engineered not only to enhance MRI contrast but also to serve other purposes, such as fluorescence imaging or drug delivery [239].

Multimodal imaging approaches employing CQDs represent a cutting-edge strategy that integrates a spectrum of imaging techniques. This innovative methodology seeks to furnish a holistic and refined comprehension of biological systems, tissues, or materials under investigation [113]. By harnessing the distinctive properties of CQDs, such as their tunable fluorescence, magnetic attributes, and potential for heavy element incorporation, researchers aim to create a versatile platform capable of delivering an unparalleled depth of information [240]. The primary advantage of multimodal imaging with CQDs lies in its ability to synergistically amalgamate the strengths of diverse imaging modalities. This synergistic integration not only broadens the scope of data acquisition but also enhances the precision and accuracy of diagnostics by providing complementary information [241]. The synergy allows researchers and clinicians to capture a more nuanced and comprehensive view of intricate biological processes, fostering a deeper understanding of the underlying mechanisms. Ongoing research endeavors in this dynamic field are dedicated to the continual refinement and optimization of CQD properties [242]. Researchers are exploring ways to fine-tune the size, surface chemistry, and functionalization of CQDs to tailor them for specific imaging tasks. Additionally, efforts are directed toward expanding the functionalities of CQDs, enabling them to serve as multifaceted imaging agents with improved stability, sensitivity, and targeting capabilities.

Bouzas-Ramos et al. used a one-pot microwave-assisted hydrothermal method to synthesize CQDs co-doped with nitrogen and lanthanides (more precisely, Gd and Yb). These CQDs were evaluated by the researchers as improved multimodal contrast agents for imaging uses. The resultant doped-CQDs showed excellent contrast qualities for computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance (MR), as well as strong fluorescence emission with remarkable quantum yields (66 ± 7 %). When these nanoparticles were exposed to three distinct cell lines for 24 and 72 h, they notably showed very little cytotoxicity. These nanoparticles' exceptional qualities make them the perfect markers for multimodal imaging. The nitrogen and lanthanide co-doped CQDs were successfully used for in vitro fluorescence, MR, and CT cell imaging to demonstrate their potential. Furthermore, after being injected intravenously into the tail vein of a mouse, these nanoparticles significantly improved the contrast between MR and CT images in the mouse's bladder and kidneys. These in vivo investigations further substantiate the nanoparticles' promising potential as contrast agents for multimodal imaging [243].

Table 4 demonstrates a comparison of various imaging methods, including Fluorescence Imaging, Photoacoustic Imaging, MRI Contrast Enhancement, and Multimodal Imaging Approaches, with a focus on their applications with CQDs:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Future and perspective of utilizing CQDs in bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: QDs have emerged as highly promising nanomaterials due to their exceptional optical properties, paving the way for groundbreaking advancements in numerous scientific and technological domains. Among their multifaceted applications, CQDs have captured considerable attention for their potential to revolutionize bioimaging techniques, thereby propelling the realms of medical diagnostics and biological exploration to unprecedented heights. Characterized by remarkable biocompatibility, minimal toxicity, and flexible surface chemistry, these carbon-based nanostructures are poised for a myriad of biomedical applications. In the realm of bioimaging, their ability to emit tunable photoluminescence, coupled with high quantum yield and remarkable photostability, renders them uniquely suited for visualizing intricate biological architectures at both the cellular and molecular scales, offering researchers unprecedented insights into the complexities of living systems.

The utilization of CQDs in bioimaging presents a highly promising perspective owing to their advantageous characteristics. Firstly, their diminutive size facilitates efficient cellular uptake and dispersion, thereby enabling the visualization of cellular processes with minimal disruption. Moreover, the ability to tune the emission wavelengths of CQDs renders them adaptable to various imaging modalities, including fluorescence and multi-modal imaging, thereby granting researchers flexibility in experimental design. The surface functionalization of CQDs further augments their specificity for targeting particular biomolecules or cellular structures, thereby enabling highly selective imaging in intricate biological milieus. Looking ahead, the ongoing development and refinement of CQDs hold significant potential for advancing bioimaging technologies. Researchers are actively investigating innovative synthetic methodologies to tailor the optical and chemical properties of CQDs, to enhance their performance and address specific imaging challenges. The integration of CQDs with other imaging agents or therapeutic agents may pave the way for the development of multifunctional nanoprobes, facilitating simultaneous imaging and targeted therapy.

Moreover, the potential use of CQDs in real-time imaging and diagnostics is an exciting prospect. As advancements in nanotechnology and materials science progress, CQDs could play a crucial role in the development of next-generation imaging platforms, providing researchers and clinicians with powerful tools for early disease detection, monitoring treatment responses, and gaining deeper insights into the intricacies of biological processes. The biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of CQDs position them as attractive candidates for in vivo imaging applications, paving the way for non-invasive imaging techniques with improved sensitivity and specificity. In conclusion, the perspective of utilizing CQDs in bioimaging is highly promising, driven by their unique optical properties, biocompatibility, and versatility. The ongoing research and development in this field are expected to lead to innovative applications, shaping the future of bioimaging technologies and contributing to advancements in the understanding and treatment of various diseases.

CQDs have emerged as promising candidates for bioimaging applications due to their excellent photoluminescent properties, biocompatibility, and low toxicity. To further enhance their utility in bioimaging, several strategies can be employed. Firstly, surface functionalization plays a crucial role in improving the biocompatibility and targeting ability of CQDs. Functional groups such as amino, carboxyl, or hydroxyl can be introduced onto the surface of CQDs to impart them with specific functionalities, enabling targeted imaging of particular biomolecules or organelles within living cells. Additionally, surface modification can also enhance the dispersibility of CQDs in aqueous solutions, ensuring their stability and minimizing nonspecific interactions with biological components. Secondly, the synthesis of CQDs with tunable photoluminescence properties is essential for bioimaging applications. By controlling the size, morphology, and surface chemistry during the synthesis process, researchers can tailor the optical properties of CQDs to match specific imaging requirements. For instance, adjusting the size of CQDs can modulate their emission wavelength, allowing for multicolor imaging or compatibility with different imaging modalities such as fluorescence microscopy or photoacoustic imaging. Furthermore, efforts to improve the photostability and quantum yield of CQDs are essential for long-term and high-resolution bioimaging. Strategies such as passivation of surface defects, doping with heteroatoms, or encapsulation within inert matrices can minimize photobleaching and enhance the brightness of CQDs, enabling prolonged imaging sessions with minimal signal degradation. Moreover, the development of novel synthesis techniques, such as microwave-assisted synthesis or hydrothermal methods, can yield CQDs with superior optical properties and stability compared to conventional approaches. In addition to enhancing the intrinsic properties of CQDs, their integration with advanced imaging technologies can further expand their utility in bioimaging. For instance, coupling CQDs with advanced imaging modalities like super-resolution microscopy or multiphoton imaging can facilitate high-resolution imaging of biological structures at the nanoscale level. Moreover, incorporating CQDs into multifunctional nanocomposites or hybrid imaging probes can enable simultaneous imaging and therapy, opening up new possibilities for theragnostic applications in biomedicine.

This comprehensive review has provided a thorough exploration of the exciting realm of CQDs, focusing on their green synthesis, characterization techniques, and burgeoning applications in bioimaging. The green synthesis methods highlighted underscore the growing importance of sustainable and environmentally friendly approaches, paving the way for eco-conscious material synthesis. The detailed examination of characterization techniques has shed light on the diverse structural and optical properties of CQDs, which are essential for tailoring their performance in various applications. Furthermore, the discussion on bioimaging applications emphasizes the immense potential of CQDs as versatile and efficient probes for visualizing biological systems. The unique optical properties, biocompatibility, and ease of functionalization make CQDs promising candidates for advancing the field of bioimaging. Finally, this review serves as a valuable resource for researchers, and scientists, offering insights into the current state of CQDs research and paving the way for future developments in sustainable synthesis, advanced characterization techniques, and groundbreaking bioimaging applications. The multifaceted nature of CQDs ensures their continued significance in diverse scientific disciplines, promising a future where these nanomaterials play a pivotal role in shaping the landscape of materials science and biomedical research.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shelesh Krishna Saraswat is an esteemed Assistant Professor in the Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering at GLA University. His expertises, coupled with his dynamic teaching style, has earned him the respect and admiration of both students and colleagues alike. Having earned his academic credentials through rigorous study and research, Dr. Saraswat brings a wealth of knowledge and practical experience to his role as Assistant Professor.

Mohammed Ahmed Mustafa is a distinguished professional in the field of Nano and Medical Technology. With an extensive academic background and a wealth of experience, Dr. Mustafa has made significant contributions to the University of Imam Jaafar AL-Sadiq. Having earned his doctorate in Medical Laboratory Technology, Dr. Mustafa has dedicated himself to advancing the field through his expertise and leadership. His research and publications have earned him recognition within the academic community, and his commitment to educating future generations of medical laboratory professionals has made a lasting impact on the University of Imam Jaafar AL-Sadiq.

Ghadir Kamil Ghadir is a distinguished researcher and academic who has made significant contributions to the sciences. Throughout his career, Dr. Ghadir has focused on the development and application of nanostructures, such as quantum dots. His work has extended into diverse areas, including drug delivery systems and photovoltaic technology. Dr. Ghadir Kamil Ghadir’s work has had a profound impact on the field, and his commitment to innovation and education has solidified his reputation as a leading figure in the exploration of nanostructures and their wide-ranging implications.

Mandeep Kaur is a distinguished academic and researcher in the field of Chemistry, currently serving as a key faculty member at the Department of Chemistry within the School of Sciences at Jain (Deemed-to-be) University in Bengaluru, India. With a passion for advancing scientific knowledge and a dedication to educating the next generation of scientists, Dr. Kaur’s contributions to the university and the field of Chemistry are widely recognized.

Darío Fernando Guamán Lozada is a distinguished academic and professional at the Escuela Superior Politécnica de Chimborazo. His dedication to education and innovation has left an indelible mark on the academic and professional communityes, and he continues to inspire and mentor many aspiring students.

Mahmood Hasen Shuhata Alubiady is a distinguished professional in the field of medical engineering and nanotechnology, currently serving as a faculty member at the Department of Medical Engineering at Al-Hadi University College. With a strong passion for merging the realms of medicine, Dr. Alubiady has dedicated his career to advancing technology and innovation in the healthcare sector and nanotechnology.

Ahmed Muzahem Al-Ani is a renowned figure, currently serving as a faculty member at the Department of Medical Engineering at Al-Nisour University College. With a passion for medical technologies in nanotechnology and a commitment to advancing healthcare solutions, Dr. Al-Ani has dedicated his career to teaching and researching the intersection of these fields.

Mohammad Y Alshahrani possesses expertise in the field of Clinical Laboratory Sciences, complemented by a deep understanding of nanotechnology. His proficiency in nanotechnology allows him to explore the integration of cutting-edge advancements in nanomedicine and nanobiotechnology into the realm of clinical laboratory sciences. Dr. Alshahrani’s interdisciplinary skills enable him to contribute to research and innovation at the intersection of nanotechnology and medical diagnostics, paving the way for new possibilities in disease detection, drug delivery systems, and molecular imaging techniques within the healthcare sector.

Mohammed Kadhem Abid is a dedicated and accomplished professional in the field of nanotechnology and medical technology. With a strong educational background from the College of Health & Medical Technology at Al-Ayen University, Dr. Abid has honed his skills in both theoretical knowledge and practical applications in these advanced fields.

Sally Salih Jumaa is an esteemed figure in the field of medical engineering, currently serving as a faculty member at the Department of Medical Engineering at the National University of Science and Technology. With a profound expertise in the intersection of medical engineering and nanotechnology, Dr. Jumaa has dedicated her career to pioneering research and innovative applications in this specialized area.

Dheyaa Yahaia Alhameedi is a distinguished figure in the field of nanotechnology, with a primary focus on health and medical technology. Dr. Alhameedi has a profound understanding of how nanotechnology can be applied to revolutionize healthcare, offering innovative solutions for diagnosis, treatment, and drug delivery. Throughout his career, Dr. Alhameedi has demonstrated a remarkable ability to bridge the gap between nanotechnology and medical sciences, pioneering groundbreaking research and developments in the field. His skills and knowledge have enabled him to contribute significantly to the advancement of medical technology, often pushing the boundaries of what is considered possible in the realm of healthcare.

Ahmed Huseen Redhee is a prominent figure in the field of medical techniques . Dr. Redhee’s passion for innovation led him to specialize in the application of nanotechnology in medical diagnostics. Through his research and practical applications, Dr. Redhee has significantly contributed to the intersection of medical science and nanotechnology.
